SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper provides a focused experimental comparison against relevant baselines (the supervised and semi-supervised methods of Gutiérrez et al. (2016)) and tests multiple subspace-selection techniques (five variants), which supports the core claims about effectiveness. However, important reproducibility details are missing (hyperparameters not reported, code not mentioned), and no failure cases or limitations are discussed; the evaluation also appears limited to a single adjective–noun dataset, leaving generality underexplored. Related work coverage is reported as thorough, but the absence of detailed experimental/sensitivity analysis and reproducibility information prevents a higher rating. | B(3): The paper supports its main claims with quantitative comparisons to relevant baselines (supervised and semi‑supervised tensor methods) and experiments on multiple subspace selection techniques, but evaluation is limited to a single adjective–noun dataset (Gutiérrez et al.) which constrains generality. Important reproducibility details are missing (no hyperparameters reported, code not mentioned), and the paper does not discuss failure cases or limitations. Related work coverage is described as thorough, but ablation/sensitivity analyses appear partial (some exploration of five selection techniques but no systematic hyperparameter or robustness studies).

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper introduces a distinct modeling perspective—constructing a geometric, context-sensitive subspace for every candidate adjective-noun pair that projects independent word vectors and enables zero-shot identification of unseen metaphors. This approach is clearly differentiated from prior tensor-based, adjective-specific methods (Gutiérrez et al. 2016) and is a non-trivial technical departure (no phrase representations, only individual word vectors). Because metaphor identification is a well-studied task, the contribution is notable and informative to experts but not a radical paradigm shift, warranting a score of 4. | B(4): The paper introduces a notable and clearly differentiated idea: a geometric, context-sensitive model that generates an ad hoc subspace for each adjective–noun pair using only independent PMI-based word vectors, enabling zero-shot identification of unseen metaphors. This contrasts with prior tensor-based supervised and semi-supervised methods (Gutiérrez et al. 2016) and is empirically competitive while relying on simpler inputs and a different geometric perspective. The technique is a non-trivial extension of distributional approaches rather than a trivial recombination of known methods, but it stops short of a highly groundbreaking theoretical advance, so its novelty is solid but not maximal.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): This paper addresses a core NLP/ML problem—metaphor identification using distributional semantics—and introduces a zero-shot, context-sensitive geometric model evaluated on an established adjective-noun dataset (8,592 dyads) with PMI vectors built from English Wikipedia, so the topic and audience align well with top NLP/ML venues. The methodology (constructing subspaces from independent word vectors and logistic regression classification) and comparisons to strong baselines (Gutiérrez et al. 2016 variants and word2vec cosine) match expectations for methods and evaluation. Minor concerns that temper a perfect fit are the limited adjective coverage (23 adjectives), missing details such as hyperparameters and code availability, and no stated limitations, which are issues reviewers at top venues commonly flag. | B(5): This paper addresses metaphor identification using distributional semantics and a zero-shot geometric model, which sits squarely within the scope of top NLP/ML venues. It evaluates on a standard adjective-noun dataset (Gutiérrez et al. 2016), compares to appropriate baselines (supervised and semi-supervised tensor methods, cosine similarity), and reports F-scores—i.e., it uses methods and evaluation standards expected by the community. The problem formulation (contextual subspace projection for unseen metaphors) targets the right audience and is positioned with thorough related work coverage, making it a strong fit for a top conference.

The methodology (constructing subspaces from independent word vectors and logistic regression classification) and comparisons to strong baselines (Gutiérrez et al. 2016 variants and word2vec cosine) match expectations for methods and evaluation. SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper's central experimental claim—that a contextually generated geometric subspace plus logistic regression attains F-scores comparable to Gutiérrez et al. (2016) and better than their semi‑supervised variant—is supported by reported experiments on the Gutiérrez adjective‑noun dataset using a PMI Wiki space and appropriate baseline comparisons. However, key methodological details are missing (no hyperparameter reporting, no code availability) and no statistical significance testing is mentioned, which weakens the empirical support and reproducibility. Mathematical/declarative aspects of the geometric model are not reported as formal derivations or proofs in the summary, so correctness of those theoretical claims cannot be verified. The blanket “state‑of‑the‑art” and zero‑shot generalization claims are therefore incompletely justified given the limited set of baselines and missing experimental details. | B(3): The paper provides empirical evidence supporting its main claims by evaluating the proposed PMI-based, contextually generated subspace method on the Gutiérrez et al. adjective-noun dataset and comparing against the supervised and semi-supervised tensor baselines. However, important methodological details are missing (hyperparameter settings not reported, no statistical significance tests, code not provided), and the evaluation is limited to 23 adjectives which weakens broad zero-shot/state-of-the-art claims. These omissions create concerns about reproducibility and the strength of the claimed generalization, so the technical soundness is plausible but not fully established.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper's central experimental claim—that a contextually generated geometric subspace plus logistic regression attains F-scores comparable to Gutiérrez et al. However, key methodological details are missing (no hyperparameter reporting, no code availability) and no statistical significance testing is mentioned, which weakens the empirical support and reproducibility. The blanket “state‑of‑the‑art” and zero‑shot generalization claims are therefore incompletely justified given the limited set of baselines and missing experimental details.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper appears well-organized and follows a clear progression from method to experiments and conclusion, with the main contributions stated early (geometric contextual model for zero-shot metaphor identification) and a thorough related-work section. Writing quality is noted as good and the methodology is described in sufficient detail in the summary (PMI-based vectors, subspace selection, logistic regression), suggesting consistent presentation. Minor presentation gaps reduce clarity slightly: hyperparameters are not reported, no limitations are stated, and code availability is not mentioned, which should be addressed for reproducibility and reader comprehension. | B(4): The summary indicates a well-organized paper with clear main contributions stated early, a coherent methodology section (corpus, vector space, subspace selection, classification), and generally good writing and thorough related-work coverage. Presentation is weakened by missing details that affect reproducibility and clarity (no hyperparameters reported, no code mentioned, and no stated limitations), and the summary does not indicate whether figures/tables or notation are fully defined. Overall the paper reads well but has minor presentation gaps that prevent a top score.

Overall the paper reads well but has minor presentation gaps that prevent a top score.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper targets the important problem of metaphor identification, specifically zero-shot detection of unseen adjective–noun metaphors, which is a notable challenge in NLP. Its claim—that a simple, interpretable geometric, context-sensitive model using only individual word vectors achieves F-scores comparable to a supervised tensor method and superior to a semi-supervised variant, obtaining state-of-the-art results on the Gutiérrez et al. dataset—suggests results that could shift attention toward lightweight, generalizable approaches. The approach’s simplicity and the proposal of ad hoc contextual subspaces open clear new research directions (contextual geometric representations and interpretability) and make it likely others will build on it for related compositional and metaphory tasks. | B(4): The paper tackles the important problem of zero-shot identification of unseen metaphors using only individual word vectors and a contextually generated geometric subspace, which could change how researchers approach metaphor detection without phrase-specific training. It reports state-of-the-art performance on the Gutiérrez et al. (2016) adjective–noun dataset and achieves F-scores comparable to a supervised tensor-based method while outperforming a semi-supervised variant, indicating practical impact. However, evaluation appears confined to a single adjective–noun dataset (8,592 dyads, 23 adjectives) and the summary omits code and hyperparameter details, limiting immediate broad adoption and preventing the work from being fully transformative.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper targets the important problem of metaphor identification, specifically zero-shot detection of unseen adjective–noun metaphors, which is a notable challenge in NLP. Its claim—that a simple, interpretable geometric, context-sensitive model using only individual word vectors achieves F-scores comparable to a supervised tensor method and superior to a semi-supervised variant, obtaining state-of-the-art results on the Gutiérrez et al. dataset—suggests results that could shift attention toward lightweight, generalizable approaches. The approach’s simplicity and the proposal of ad hoc contextual subspaces open clear new research directions (contextual geometric representations and interpretability) and make it likely others will build on it for related compositional and metaphory tasks.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper tackles the important problem of zero-shot identification of unseen metaphors using only individual word vectors and a contextually generated geometric subspace, which could change how researchers approach metaphor detection without phrase-specific training. It reports state-of-the-art performance on the Gutiérrez et al. (2016) adjective–noun dataset and achieves F-scores comparable to a supervised tensor-based method while outperforming a semi-supervised variant, indicating practical impact. However, evaluation appears confined to a single adjective–noun dataset (8,592 dyads, 23 adjectives) and the summary omits code and hyperparameter details, limiting immediate broad adoption and preventing the work from being fully transformative.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against the key prior work (Gutiérrez et al. 2016 supervised and semi‑supervised tensor methods) plus simple baselines (majority, word2vec logistic regression) on the same Gutiérrez adjective‑noun dataset and evaluates with F‑score. It acknowledges when prior methods perform comparably or worse and explains methodological differences (geometric context subspaces vs tensor models), and the related‑work coverage is reported as thorough. Minor shortcomings reduce completeness: hyperparameters are not reported and there is no evidence of comparisons to other recent neural/SOTA baselines, so the comparisons are good but not fully comprehensive. | B(4): The paper compares against appropriate and recent baselines (Gutiérrez et al. 2016 supervised and semi-supervised tensor methods, a majority baseline, and a cosine word2vec logistic baseline) and evaluates on the same adjective-noun dataset using F-score. It explicitly acknowledges when prior methods perform comparably (reports F-scores comparable to the supervised tensor method and superior to the semi-supervised variant) and contrasts its approach (per-pair subspaces from individual word vectors, zero-shot capability) with tensor-based baselines. Related work coverage is described as thorough. Minor omissions that prevent a full top score: hyperparameter reporting and code availability are not mentioned, leaving some fairness/reproducibility details unclear.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against the key prior work (Gutiérrez et al. 2016 supervised and semi‑supervised tensor methods) plus simple baselines (majority, word2vec logistic regression) on the same Gutiérrez adjective‑noun dataset and evaluates with F‑score. It acknowledges when prior methods perform comparably or worse and explains methodological differences (geometric context subspaces vs tensor models), and the related‑work coverage is reported as thorough. Minor shortcomings reduce completeness: hyperparameters are not reported and there is no evidence of comparisons to other recent neural/SOTA baselines, so the comparisons are good but not fully comprehensive.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against appropriate and recent baselines (Gutiérrez et al. 2016 supervised and semi-supervised tensor methods, a majority baseline, and a cosine word2vec logistic baseline) and evaluates on the same adjective-noun dataset using F-score. It explicitly acknowledges when prior methods perform comparably (reports F-scores comparable to the supervised tensor method and superior to the semi-supervised variant) and contrasts its approach (per-pair subspaces from individual word vectors, zero-shot capability) with tensor-based baselines. Related work coverage is described as thorough. Minor omissions that prevent a full top score: hyperparameter reporting and code availability are not mentioned, leaving some fairness/reproducibility details unclear.

main_claims: Proposes a geometric, context-sensitive model that generates a new subspace for every candidate adjective-noun pair, projecting word pairs into interpretable geometric relationships based on independent word vectors.
The model requires only individual word vectors (no phrase representations) and is suitable for zero-shot identification of novel, unseen metaphors.

Using logistic regression over features from the contextually generated subspaces, the method achieves F-scores comparable to the supervised tensor-based method of Gutiérrez et al. (2016) and superior to their semi-supervised variant.
The approach is simple and general, and is reported to obtain state-of-the-art performance on the tested adjective-noun metaphoricity identification task.
_summary.methodology: Build a standard PMI-based distributional vector space from a large corpus (December 2014 English Wikipedia), then for each candidate adjective-noun pair select k-dimensional subspaces (five selection techniques experimented) projecting the independent word vectors into an ad hoc geometric space; classify metaphoric vs literal uses via logistic regression on features derived from these subspaces.
datasets: Gutiérrez et al. (2016) adjective-noun dataset (8,592 adjective-noun dyads, 23 adjectives)
English Wikipedia (December 2014 dump) for building PMI co-occurrence vectors
baselines: Gutiérrez et al. (2016) supervised adjective-tensor method
Gutiérrez et al. (2016) semi-supervised adjective-tensor method
Logistic regression using cosine similarity from word2vec
